Main Title: Report on the eight-hours day or forty-eight hours week
Responsibility: prepared by the Organising Committee for the International Labour Conference, Washington, 1919.
Abstract/Summary: Presents replies to a questionnaire received from 27 member States concerning legislation on the limitation of hours of work to eight hours a day or 48 hours a week.

Main Title: Report on the employment of women and children and the Berne conventions of 1906
Responsibility: prepared by the Organising Committee for the International Labour Conference, Washington, 1919.
Abstract/Summary: Summarizes national laws and regulations in the form of synoptic tables. Includes the text of the Berne Convention of 1906 on the prohibition of night work for women in industrial employment and on the prohibition of the use of White (yellow) phosphorus in the manufacture of matches.

Main Title: Report on unemployment /
Responsibility: prepared by the Organising Committee for the International Labour conference, Washington, 1919.
Abstract/Summary: Presents replies to a questionnaire received from 27 member States to determine the nature and extent of unemployment and measures taken for the prevention of unemployment and for provision against it.

Main Title: Report on unemployment
Responsibility: prepared by the Organising Committee for the International Labour conference, Washington, 1919.
Abstract/Summary: Presents replies to a questionnaire received from 27 member States to determine the nature and extent of unemployment and measures taken for the prevention of unemployment and for provision against it.

Main Title: Supplemental report on certain countries for which information was received too late for inclusion in reports I, II and III
Responsibility: prepared by the Organising Committee for the International Labour Conference, Washington, 1919.
Abstract/Summary: Presents replies to the questionnaire send out by the Organising Committee and received from eight member States on the questions of the 8 hour day/48 hour week, Unemployment, Women's employment, Employment of children and the Bern Convention, 1906.

Main Title: 15th congress of the general confederation of labour ( confederation generale du travail) (France), held at orleans the 27th September to the 2nd October, 1920.
Abstract/Summary: Report on the trade union federation policy in France, with excerpts from a C.G.T. Conference report, 1920 - discusses nationalization of railway transport, recent strike action of railway workers, problems of joint consultation in economic administration, international relations with ILO and international labour movement militancy. Conference held in Orleans 1920 Sep 27 to Oct 2.

Main Title: 4th international congress of bookbinders.
Abstract/Summary: Conference report of the international trade union of bookbinders in the printing industry, 1920 - includes resumption of international relations; examines questions of expatriate workers' trade union affiliation, financial aspects, and transfer of the international secretariat to Switzerland. Conference held in Berne 1920 Sep 28 to 30.

Main Title: Action of the Swiss government in dealing with unemployment.
Abstract/Summary: Study of the employment policy for emergency relief in Switzerland, 1917 to 1920, to deal with unemployment caused by the First World War - describes the creation of a Federal Unemployment Fund (subsidies), a Federal Unemployment Relief Office (public works programme for employment creation), a contingency fund for unemployed nationals and foreigners, and plans for the gradual suppression of the relief measures.

Main Title: Admission of Germany and Austria to the International Labour Organisation.
Abstract/Summary: Record of the admission of Germany and Austria to the ILO as member states in 1919.

Main Title: Agreement between the Spanish workers' organisations.
Abstract/Summary: Memorandum on the agreement between the two central trade union federations: the Central Union of Workers and (the National Confederation of Labour in Spain, 1920 - gives the conditions and circumstances of this agreement in a Manifesto published in the organ of the socialist party; explains that in spite of their differences (the National Confederation being affiliated to the Communist International) the two organisations decided to move towards an amalgamation in order to influence the social policy, protect the working class and defend their civil rights.

Main Title: Agricultural questions: questionnaire.
Abstract/Summary: Based on data from published reports, government replies to a questionnaire and special documents prepared by certain countries. Provides information on legislation concerning employment issues in the agricultural sector in various member States.

Main Title: Annual meeting of the trades union congress 1920.
Abstract/Summary: Conference report on the 52nd annual meeting of UK trade union federation (TUC) in 1920 - discusses the miners labour dispute, creation of a council of action in relation with the USSR, trade union structure, relations with the socialist political party, affiliation to the international trade union movement, etc.; gives ILOs discussions on maternity benefit and unemployment, and hours of work of seafarers and agricultural workers; examines problems such as cost of living, housing, education, etc. Conference held in Portsmouth 1920 Sep 6 to 11.

Main Title: British government and the miners' federation of Great Britain.
Abstract/Summary: Conference report on the UK coal miners' strike of 1920, and discussions held between the President of the Board of Trade and the representative of the miners' federation - centres on the miners' claims for reduction in coal prices, and wage increase for shift work at differencial rates for men, young workers and boys (child labour). Conference held in London 1920 Jul 6.

Main Title: British government and the miners' federation of Great Britain.
Abstract/Summary: Verbatim report of negociation between representatives of government and the trade union federation of miners, railway workers and transport workers prior to the 1920 coal mining strike in the UK - (cf. "Studies and Reports", Series A no. 5); centres on failure of collective bargaining concerning wage increase, price maintenance for household coal and the creation of a commission to study questions of production control and wage payment system.

Main Title: British legislation on unemployment insurance.
Abstract/Summary: Brief summary and comments on UK labour legislation on unemployment benefit, 1920 - summarises provisions of the 1920 Act; reviews the beginnings of unemployment insurance in the 1911 Act and its extension in the 1916 Act; shows that the 1920 Act provides for public scheme administered through employment services and a private scheme for members of trade unions, special schemes whereby industry may contract out of the general scheme and supplementary schemes which allow the rate of contribution and benefits to be adapted to the risks of each industry.

Main Title: Bulgarian law on compulsory labour.
Abstract/Summary: Study of the Bulgarian labour legislation on compulsory civic service (forced labour) to promote the economic reconstruction, 1920 - explains general features and administrative aspects of the system; surveys regulations applying to youth, men, women and girls; contains list of relevant articles.

Main Title: Coal production in the ruhr district (end of may, 1920); enquiry by the International Labour Office.
Abstract/Summary: Study of hours of work, wages and economic recovery in coal mining, Germany (the Ruhr), 1920 - reviews production increase, food security problems, cost of living trends and labour dispute; studies policies of five principal trade unions with regard to reorganisation of workers representation and nationalization of the mines.

Main Title: Conditions of labour and production in the upper silesian coalfield.
Abstract/Summary: Study on working conditions, economic conditions and productivity in coal mining in Germany (Upper Silesia), 1920 - gives a brief overview of labour disputes, political problems and trade union activity since 1918 (hours of work, wages, food rationing, shift work, etc.); identifies the preconditions of industrial growth as labour supply increase, housing provision, employment service, technological change and capital investment.

Main Title: Congress of the labour and socialist international (Geneva, July 31st-August 6th, 1920).
Abstract/Summary: Conference report on the congress of the Second International (socialist political party), 1920 - explains the impact of socialism on international trade union and the influence of the Third International (communist) on Western unions; discusses socialist emulation, resolutions voted on international unity for unionized workers, the problem of peace and the constitution of the League of Nations, need to support the ILO, nationalization (socialisation) and the political system. Conference held in Geneva Jul 31 to Aug 6.

Main Title: Disinfection of wool infected with anthrax spores: questionnaire.
Abstract/Summary: Based on data from published reports, government replies to a questionnaire, and special documents prepared by selected countries.

Main Title: Disinfection of wool infected with anthrax spores
Subtitle: : questionnaire /
Responsibility: International Labour Organization.
Abstract/Summary: Based on data from published reports, government replies to a questionnaire, and special documents prepared by selected countries.

Main Title: Dispute in the metal industry in italy; trade union control in industry.
Abstract/Summary: Report on the dispute settlement by state intervention, the establishing workers participation in the metalworking industry and machinery industry in Italy, 1920 - (cf. "Studies and Reports, Series A no. 2"; gives text of the final agreement concerning job classification, discussion in the Senate and views of the Catholic party and the press, etc.

Main Title: Dispute in the metal industry in italy; trade union control of the industry.
Abstract/Summary: Report on a labour dispute between the trade unions, in the metalworking industry and machinery industry, and employers organizations in Italy, 1920 - presents the trade union claims concerning job classification, wage increase, wage incentive, minimum wage, overtime, night work,layoff, paid leave, etc.; describes the gravity of the conflict (lockout, occupation of factories, state intervention); discusses project of trade union control of the industry.

Main Title: Employment of children at sea.
Abstract/Summary: Based on the replies to a questionnaire addressed to the Governments of member States, reviews the law and practice with respect to minimum age for admission of children to employment at sea. Includes the draft text of the Minimum Age (Sea) Convention, 1920 (No. 7).

Main Title: Enquiry into production: introductory memorandum.
Abstract/Summary: Introductory memorandum on an ILO international survey of trends in industrial production, 1913 to 1920 - enlarges on the scope of the enquiry i.e. Facts, causes and solutions of problems relating to post 1918 economic recession, labour force, and employment; outlines the plan of enquiry; includes questionnaires for trade unions, employers organizations and cooperative societies. Graphs and statistical tables.

Main Title: Government action in dealing with unemployment in Italy.
Abstract/Summary: Study of employment policy measures for emergency relief in Italy, 1914 to 1920, to deal with unemployment caused by the First World War - describes employment of workers in the rice-fields and unemployment benefit of munitions workers during the war, subsidies for the unemployed after the armistice and the 1917 labour legislation introducing a general compulsory unemployment insurance, reorganisation of employment services and granting of evaluation for public works; gives trade union attitudes and proposed amendments to the 1919 decree.

Main Title: Hours of labour and their effect on manning and accommodation.
Abstract/Summary: Based on the replies of 15 countries to a questionnaire addressed to the governments of member States. Reviews the law and practice dealing with hours of labour for seafarers in those countries.

Main Title: International congress of metal workers..
Abstract/Summary: Conference report of the international trade union for the metalworking industry, 1920 - includes delegates from 11 countries and 16 organisations and one representative of the ILO; gives the agenda concerning resolutions on international relations and reduced hours of work, reorganisation of the federation and revision of its trade union constitution. Conference held in Copenhagen 1920 Aug 20 to 26.

Main Title: International congress of workers in the food and drink trades.
Abstract/Summary: Conference report on the international congress of food workers trade union federations, 1920 - lists delegates from Western Europe, Hungary, Czechoslovakia, North America and a representative of the ILO; gives the agenda incl. A proposal for creation an international federation of workers in the food and drink trades (bakers, butchers, Hotel workers, etc.), discussion on the constitution of the union, etc.; includes texts of three resolutions voted on night work, the USSR and socialist organisation of industry. Conference held in Zurich 1920 Aug 25 to 27.

Main Title: International Labour Conference first annual meeting, October 29, 1919 - November 29, 1919
Subtitle: Washington, D.C., U.S.A.

Main Title: International labour organisation: a comparison.
Abstract/Summary: Comparison of the ILO and its predecessor the International Association of Labour Legislation, created ten years after the Berlin international conference on labour standards, held in 1890 - gives a brief historical review of the Association, its aims on working conditions, hours of work, woman workers, night work, labour inspection, etc., its failure and the subsequent establishment of the ILO; explains the tripartite structure of the international labour conferences; covers the development and activities of the Organisation incl. ILO Conventions

Main Title: Labour conditions in soviet Russia: systematic questionnaire and bibliography prepared for the mission of enquiry in Russia.
Abstract/Summary: Report on working conditions and labour relations in the USSR immediately after the Bolshevik Revolution, prepared for an ILO Mission and based on an extensive literature survey of documents published between 1917 and 1920 - covers hours of work, wages, woman workers, child labour, unemployment, work at home, social security, forced labour, strikes, labour administration, trade unions, nationalization, workers control, cooperatives, managerial and technicians. Extensive bibliography of ILO publications and selected annotated bibliography.

Conference held in Geneva 1920 Aug 2 to 6.

Main Title: Miners' strike in Great Britain.
Abstract/Summary: Account of the origins, progress and dispute settlement of the coal miners strike in UK in 1920 - gives a brief overview of miners' claims for wage increase, cost reduction of household coal and nationalization of coal mining; examines chronology of the strike, support of the railway workers and transport workers trade unions, collective bargaining and secret ballots; notes the social cost and benefits. Table.

Abstract/Summary: Discussion of the last paragraph of Article XVIII of the Draft convention creating a permanent organisation for the promotion of the international regulation of labour conditions  (creating the ILO) concerning measures to be taken by states to give effect to provisions of conventions.  Also discussion of articles XXII to XXIV concerning representations made against States regarding failure to observe conventions, and the right to file a complaint with the ILO.

Main Title: Articles XXIV, XV, XXVI, XXVII, XXVIII and XXIX
Responsibility: The Commission on International Labour Legislation.
Abstract/Summary: Discussion articles XXIV through XXIX of the Draft convention creating a permanent organisation for the promotion of the international regulation of labour conditions  (creating the ILO) concerning complaints, constitution of a Commission of Enquiry, information on complaints, reports and recommendations of the Commission of Enquiry, response from the States concerned in a complaint, referring matters to the International Court.

Main Title: Articles XXX, XXXI, XXXII, XXXIII, XXXIV, XXXV and XXXVI
Responsibility: The Commission on International Labour Legislation.
Abstract/Summary: Discussion articles XXX through XXXVI of the Draft convention creating a permanent organisation for the promotion of the international regulation of labour conditions  (creating the ILO) concerning decisions of the International Court, power of the International Court regarding findings or recommendation of the Commission of Enquiry, measures that any other State may take against the commerce of a defaulting State, right of a defaulting State to request constitution of a Commission of Enquiry to verify its contention, regarding the self-governing Dominions of the British Empire and India, adherence of States who become members of the League of Nations, amendments to this convention.

Main Title: Articles XXXIV, XXXVI and XXXVII
Responsibility: The Commission on International Labour Legislation.
Abstract/Summary: Continued discussion of articles XXXIV and XXXVI of the Draft convention creating a permanent organisation for the promotion of the international regulation of labour conditions  (creating the ILO) regarding the self-governing Dominions of the British Empire and India and  amendments to this convention. Discussing of article XXXVII concerning the provisions of this convention coming into force.

Main Title: Articles XXXVIII and XVIII
Responsibility: The Commission on International Labour Legislation.
Abstract/Summary: Discussion  Draft convention creating a permanent organisation for the promotion of the international regulation of labour conditions  (creating the ILO) concerning The first meeting of the Conference, on ratification of conventions by States, and on composition of the Governing Body.

Responsibility: by John Lindberg ; International Labour Office.
Abstract/Summary: Analyses statistics referring to the general movement of unemployment, calculates national indexes for some industrial countries and discusses the possibility of constructing  an international index. Advanced technology in a strategy of development.
Abstract/Summary: Conference paper prepared for an ILO Meeting, on the economic implications of technological change and innovation, with particular reference to economic development strategy in developing countries - comprises a brief survey of decision making criteria relative to the choice of technology. (Restricted). Conference held in geneva 1970 jul 1 to 3.

Main Title: Advanced technology in Brazil; a case study.
Abstract/Summary: Conference paper prepared for an ILO Meeting, comprising a case study of training programme activities connected with the computerisation of banking procedures in Brazil, illustrating the personnel management implications of technological change and automation in developing countries. Conference held in Geneva 1970 jul 1 to 3.

Main Title: Advanced technology in Colombia; a case study.
Abstract/Summary: Conference paper prepared for an ILO Meeting, comprising a case study of the production process and employment changes in a bread and pastry making firm of the food industry in Colombia illustrating the impact of technological change on productivity increase in developing countries. Conference held in Geneva 1970 jul 1 to 3.

Main Title: Advanced technology in Ethiopia - a case study.
Abstract/Summary: Conference paper prepared for an ILO Meeting comprising a case study of labour force problems in air transport in Ethiopia as an illustration of the effect of the introduction of automation and technological change on employment in developing countries - covers employees attitude toward computerised accounting and data processing, promotion opportunities for EDP personnel, wages determination, etc. Conference held in geneva 1970 jul 1 to 3.

Main Title: Advanced technology in India; a case study.
Abstract/Summary: Conference paper prepared for an ILO Meeting, comprising a case study of labour relations problems arising from the introduction of EDP in a textile industry firm in India as an illustration of the labour force effects of technological change and automation in developing countries. Conference held in Geneva 1970 jul 1 to 3.

Main Title: Advanced technology in Pakistan; a case study.
Abstract/Summary: Conference paper prepared for an ILO Meeting, comprising a case study of the labour force effects of the introduction of computers in East Pakistan (later Bangladesh) as an illustration of employment problems arising from technological change and automation in developing countries. Conference held in Geneva 1970 jul 1 to 3.

Main Title: Advanced technology in Tanzania; a case study.
Abstract/Summary: Conference paper prepared for an ILO Meeting, comprising a case study of labour relations in a metal tin-making firm of the manufacturing industry in Tanzania illustrating employment policy issues connected with technological change and automation in developing countries. (Restricted). Conference held in geneva 1970 jul 1 to 3.

Main Title: Administrative problems of employment injury benefit schemes.
Abstract/Summary: Conference paper on administrative aspects and problems of employment accident benefit schemes, with particular reference to Malaysia - discusses evolution and development of schemes, identifies major governing characteristics (social security organization of malaysia), and considers perspectives for improved systems of administration. References. Conference held in Tokyo 1979 October 3 to 12.

Main Title: Advisory Committee on Rural Development, ninth session, Geneva, 27 November - 6 December 1979 :
Subtitle: report /
Responsibility: International Labour Organisation.
Abstract/Summary: Conference report on the role of ILO in rural development - reviews agenda items relating to ILO activities in rural development since 1974 (rural employment, training, working conditions, etc.), Discusses efforts to alleviate rural area poverty and raise rural women's' status, considers the role of rural worker organizations, rural area employers organizations and rural cooperatives, and refers to relevant ILO conventions and ILO recommendations. Conference held in Geneva 1979 November 27 to December 6.

Main Title: Advisory committee on salaried employees and professional workers, eighth session, Geneva, 1981 : general report, first item on the agenda /
Responsibility: International Labour Organisation, Programme of Industrial Activities.
Abstract/Summary: Conference paper, prepared for an ILO Meeting, on action taken by member countries and role of ILO concerning working conditions and employment of nonmanual workers and office workers - covers conclusions and ILO resolutions adopted at previous sessions on wage determination, hours of work, part time employment, older worker age discrimination, work environment, labour relations, etc., And discusses development trends. References and statistical tables. Conference held in Geneva 1981 jan 13 to 22.

Main Title: Aeltere Arbeitnehmer: Arbeit und Ruhestand.
Abstract/Summary: Report prepared for the 65th international labour conference comprising replies and commentaries from governments of ILO member states to a questionnaire survey of older workers and retirement - covers answers to questions relating to age discrimination, arrangement of working time, early retirement, old age benefits, unemployment benefit, etc., And includes proposed conclusions with respect to equal opportunity, working conditions and preparation for retirement. Statistical tables. Conference held in Geneva 1979 June 6 to 27.

Main Title: Affluent years are over: emigration and development in the Yemen Arab Republic.
Abstract/Summary: Working paper on economic development impact of labour force emigration in Yemen - discusses migration trends, issues and prospects, and examines the impact on skill and labour shortages, balance of payments, economic structure, agricultural production, trade and inflation in the 1970s as well as potential threats from return migration, decline in migrant worker remittance and degree of dependence in view of migration policies alternatives. Graphs and references.

Main Title: Afghanistan: employment policy mission; project findings and recommendations.
Abstract/Summary: UNDP-ILO pub. Project report, recommendations on employment policy geared to poverty and income distribution problems and economic development in Afghanistan - presents population and labour force trends; analyses rural development approach based on agricultural development, improved water supply and energy policy, and urban development approach oriented to industrial development, wage structure, formal-informal sector; covers the role of rural women, rural public works, etc. References and statistical tables.

Main Title: Africa. Social security coverage for migrant workers in the economic community of the great lakes countries.
Abstract/Summary: Article on a treaty of 20 September 1978 between Burundi, Rwanda and zaire aimed at harmonising national social security legislation governing migrant workers - describes scheme and refers to old age benefits, survivors benefits, health insurance, compensation for occupational diseases and employment accident benefit.

Action research scheme to promote participation of rural youth in collective poultry farming, bangladesh; final evaluation report.
Abstract/Summary: OBJECTIVES: Agricultural university will have acquired experience and skills in initiating and assisting rural poor youth in participatory collective poultry farming which it can use to launch subsequent wider programmes. The participants will have improved their economic and social status. FINDINGS: Poultry farming proved uneconomic. Success was jeopardised by the lack of full-time technical advisers, and of quality feed in the local markets. OBSTACLES: Extreme poverty was a major obstacle to group formation and collective activities. The existing power structure and prevailing socio-cultural attitude of village society mitigated against women's participation in the project. LESSONS LEARNED: Project planning needs to take into account the socio-economic status of beneficiaries and existing political atmosphere. It is extremely difficult to sustain a collective spirit in a socio-political environment nuturing individualism. Availability of small volumes of low-cost poultry feed is a prerequisite to the success of small-scale farming. Strong leadership is important to sustain the group spirit.

Main Title: Action research scheme to promote participation of rural youth in collective poultry farming, bangladesh; final evaluation report.
Abstract/Summary: OBJECTIVES: Agricultural university will have acquired experience and skills in initiating and assisting rural poor youth in participatory collective poultry farming which it can use to launch subsequent wider programmes. The participants will have improved their economic and social status. FINDINGS: Poultry farming proved uneconomic. Success was jeopardised by the lack of full-time technical advisers, and of quality feed in the local markets. OBSTACLES: Extreme poverty was a major obstacle to group formation and collective activities. The existing power structure and prevailing socio-cultural attitude of village society mitigated against women's participation in the project. LESSONS LEARNED: Project planning needs to take into account the socio-economic status of beneficiaries and existing political atmosphere. It is extremely difficult to sustain a collective spirit in a socio-political environment nuturing individualism. Availability of small volumes of low-cost poultry feed is a prerequisite to the success of small-scale farming. Strong leadership is important to sustain the group spirit.

Main Title: ACOPAM project: sub-project (mau/003); evaluation report = rapport d'evaluation: projet acopam; sous-projet mau/003.
Abstract/Summary: OBJECTIVES: Promote co-operative movement in favour of women; organise autonomous co-operatives; establish criteria for a revolving fund; strengthen the national counterpart agency (DSA). FINDINGS: Objectives partly achieved. However, the project failed in its goal to improve the quality of goods, production processes and marketing. It also failed to increase income. Horticulture revenue was minimal because of the small size of the plots. The project lacked appropriate water supplies and extension services, and experienced preservation and marketing problems. Inappropriate choice of economic activities and marketing difficulties kept the overall return of artisanal goods produced below the minimum wage levels. Benefits were limited and disproportionate to resources invested. Post-project sustainability of the pre-cooperatives considered doubtful. DESIGN WEAKNESSES: Failure to carry out market research, establish criteria for economic activities, identify groups to be assisted, and define training. There was limited target group participation in design and implementation. OBSTACLES: Frequent change of counterparts. Lack of qualified DSA staff and problems between DSA leadership and ILO/ACOPAM expert. LESSONS LEARNED: Production of handicraft goods in similar socio-economic climates should focus on utility items.

Main Title: ACOPAM project: sub-project (mau/003); evaluation report = rapport d'evaluation: projet acopam; sous-projet mau/003.
Abstract/Summary: OBJECTIVES: Promote co-operative movement in favour of women; organise autonomous co-operatives; establish criteria for a revolving fund; strengthen the national counterpart agency (DSA). FINDINGS: Objectives partly achieved. However, the project failed in its goal to improve the quality of goods, production processes and marketing. It also failed to increase income. Horticulture revenue was minimal because of the small size of the plots. The project lacked appropriate water supplies and extension services, and experienced preservation and marketing problems. Inappropriate choice of economic activities and marketing difficulties kept the overall return of artisanal goods produced below the minimum wage levels. Benefits were limited and disproportionate to resources invested. Post-project sustainability of the pre-cooperatives considered doubtful. DESIGN WEAKNESSES: Failure to carry out market research, establish criteria for economic activities, identify groups to be assisted, and define training. There was limited target group participation in design and implementation. OBSTACLES: Frequent change of counterparts. Lack of qualified DSA staff and problems between DSA leadership and ILO/ACOPAM expert. LESSONS LEARNED: Production of handicraft goods in similar socio-economic climates should focus on utility items.

Responsibility: by Francesco G. Campagna and Clifton P. Campbell ; International Labour Office ; Vocational Training Branch.
Abstract/Summary: Instructional materials are tangible trainee-oriented resources, with an instructional content of function, used for training purposes. Such materials should not be confused with instructional methods which are approaches or techniques for delivering instruction, for example, the demonstration method.  The purposes of instructional materials are to: (a) complement or supplement the instructional process; and (b) to increase training effectiveness so that trainees acquire the knowledge and skills essential for successful job performance.  However, instructional materials cannot be totally separated from the approaches or techniques for delivering instruction.

Main Title: Action to assist rural women in Tanzania; mid-term evaluation report.
Abstract/Summary: OBJECTIVES: Rural women's groups capable of organising themselves to improve their income and employment opportunities, better their working and living conditions and enhance their participation in local level decision-making bodies. The Mufindi District community development dept. capable of assisting rural women's groups in identifying and managing productive activities and replicating similar assistance in the district. FINDINGS: Inspite of major constraints, training has been given to community development assistants in technical and pedagogical skills and to women's groups leaders in group animation, management and general orientation in planning, and productive activities. Groups of women have been trained by extension workers and skills trainers. Selected women have been trained in technical fields. Workshops have been held on management skills for microenterprises. Constraints include the delay in the recruitment of the national project coordinator and field staff and in the provision of a project office in Mufindi District hampering collaboration between ILO project staff and staff from the district, integration of project activities with district activities and supervision of village activities. RECOMMENDS: Immediate transfer of project office to Mufindi District, assignment of permanent district officers to the project, assessment and planning workshops and an ILO technical backstopping mission within 3 months. Regional and District Community Development Officers should enhance managerial support to the project. Involvement of national/local expertise in training and participatory approaches in social animation and rural women's development. LESSONS LEARNED: The project's success is greatly influenced by the physical proximity of international staff to national staff. Integration of project operations into the operational framework of the national agency and involvement of the agency in planning and implementation is crucial to the continuation of project activities. In the beginning, a project should not rely solely on counterpart funds and manpower for core or essential activities if they cannot be guaranteed.

Action to assist rural women in Tanzania; mid-term evaluation report.
Abstract/Summary: OBJECTIVES: Rural women's groups capable of organising themselves to improve their income and employment opportunities, better their working and living conditions and enhance their participation in local level decision-making bodies. The Mufindi District community development dept. capable of assisting rural women's groups in identifying and managing productive activities and replicating similar assistance in the district. FINDINGS: Inspite of major constraints, training has been given to community development assistants in technical and pedagogical skills and to women's groups leaders in group animation, management and general orientation in planning, and productive activities. Groups of women have been trained by extension workers and skills trainers. Selected women have been trained in technical fields. Workshops have been held on management skills for microenterprises. Constraints include the delay in the recruitment of the national project coordinator and field staff and in the provision of a project office in Mufindi District hampering collaboration between ILO project staff and staff from the district, integration of project activities with district activities and supervision of village activities. RECOMMENDS: Immediate transfer of project office to Mufindi District, assignment of permanent district officers to the project, assessment and planning workshops and an ILO technical backstopping mission within 3 months. Regional and District Community Development Officers should enhance managerial support to the project. Involvement of national/local expertise in training and participatory approaches in social animation and rural women's development. LESSONS LEARNED: The project's success is greatly influenced by the physical proximity of international staff to national staff. Integration of project operations into the operational framework of the national agency and involvement of the agency in planning and implementation is crucial to the continuation of project activities. In the beginning, a project should not rely solely on counterpart funds and manpower for core or essential activities if they cannot be guaranteed.

Main Title: Action to assist rural women in zimbabwe; report of the final evaluation mission.
Abstract/Summary: OBJECTIVES: Rural women's groups increase their production, marketing and income from productive activities; women's groups self-management and control of resources strengthened; village, ward and district extension workers, community development workers and skills trainers, regularly assist the productive activities of women's groups; local, provincial and national officials capable of planning and managing projects supporting women's activities. FINDINGS: Several of the 32 groups assisted have improved their business performance. Progress has been made in terms of business management concepts, self-management and access to production-related resources. A training programme has been developed and implemented to improve the business management skills of rural income-generating groups and integrated into the Ministry of Community and Cooperative Development (MCCD), thus strengthening the capability of the Ministry to support income-generating groups. The institutional and operational framework of a Loan Guarantee Fund has been set up promoting rural clients' access to the banking system. RECOMMENDS: Project extension to allow for the completion of outstanding tasks including implementation of the Loan Guarantee Fund. Recommends measures to strengthen the project activities, and to develop appropriate monitoring and evalution instruments and systems for the Loan Fund, training of extension staff and further orientation of the Ministry and Bank of Zimbabwe staff on rural women's role and status in business. Also recommends testing possibilities of replicating the project in conjunction with MCCD. LESSONS LEARNED: The system put in place by the project to enable the banking structure to cater for the financial needs of rural clients (Loan Guarantee Fund with ZIMBANK) is a good alternative to the negative experience with revolving loan funds.

Action to assist rural women in Tanzania; evaluation report, revolving loan fund.
Abstract/Summary: OBJECTIVES: Rural women's groups with capacity to organise themselves to improve their income and employment opportunities, better their working and living conditions and enhance their participation in local level decision-making bodies. FINDINGS: The evaluation was based on the implementation of the Revolving Loan Fund component. The project provided credit to 46 womens' groups in 5 villages. The District Credit Committee (DCC) of the District Council (DC) recommended loan applicants to the Cooperative and Rural Development Bank (CRDB) for funding and supervised and monitored the implementation of the projects. The groups increased their food supply, incomes and access to credit and made an average loan repayment of 72 per cent. Peer pressure played an important role in ensuring repayments. Groups need to increase output per acre or diversify into non-farm activities to improve their chances of sustainability. Problems included delays in disbursing credit, the collapse of the crop marketing system and the lack of a collection account. Ineffective monitoring by the DCC resulted in the project team implementing the project almost in isolation. Sustainability also threatened by the DC's lack of funds to take over the project and the absence of an NGO to carry on the activities. RECOMMENDS: Improving the administration of the Revolving Loan Fund; encouraging women to improve their crop yields and to diversify into non-farm, off-season activities; training programmes for District and CRDB officials in managing credit schemes for the rural poor; and identifying an NGO to sustain the project's activities. LESSONS LEARNED: An evaluation mechanism built into a Revolving Loan Fund agreement ensures the Fund can be revised and improved, if necessary. The chances of a project's activities being sustained can be increased if links are made early on during implementation with NGOs which could take over from the project when it terminates.

Main Title: Action to assist rural women in zimbabwe; report of the final evaluation mission.
Abstract/Summary: OBJECTIVES: Rural women's groups increase their production, marketing and income from productive activities; women's groups self-management and control of resources strengthened; village, ward and district extension workers, community development workers and skills trainers, regularly assist the productive activities of women's groups; local, provincial and national officials capable of planning and managing projects supporting women's activities. Main Title: Action to assist rural women: Tanzania; report of the final evaluation.
Abstract/Summary: OBJECTIVES: Rural women's groups with the capacity to organise themselves in order to improve their income and employment opportunities, better their working and living conditions and enhance their participation in local level decision-making. The Community Development Department (CDD) in Mufundi District with the capacity to assist rural women's groups in identifying and managing productive activities and replicate similar assistance in the district. FINDINGS: Although the project has not fully achieved its objectives, the increase in women's incomes (achieved by providing them with access to credit, land and livestock) and official business registration of the women's groups has improved their social and economic status. In addition, executive committees set up by the project may eventually develop into sustainable means of self-management for the women's groups. The institution building aspect of the project is unlikely to be sustainable due to lack of CDD capacity in micro-enterprise development and non-farm activities and also to reductions in the civil service. Furthermore, the Cooperative Rural Development Bank which administers the revolving fund is undergoing restructuring which in future will place it out of reach of the women's groups. RECOMMENDS: Second 5-year phase concentrating on the grassroots level and on developing a District Programme of Action to Assist Rural Women. The strategy should ensure that the executive committees are an integral part of the project and that the women's representatives are provided with management training. A more concerted effort should also be made to build gender awareness. LESSONS LEARNED: The key factors for change in this project were linked to political reforms in Tanzania including privatization, land registration and local government elections. An important lesson learned was the need for flexibility within the context of the project to keep up with the changes and to contribute to the project's overall development objective. The institution building component of the project should not have been limited to just one department. The approach should have been broader and and should have included other government departments, local institutions and NGOs. This may have helped offset any threat to the sustainability of its activities during government restructuring.

Subtitle: international perspectives.

Subtitle: an ILO code of practice.
Abstract/Summary: A code to cover every aspect of safety both at work and in off-duty periods aboard ship at sea and in port, and no human activity if free from some measure of risk.  Accidents are in many cases cause by lack of knowledge or inadequate training, incomplete understanding of ships and sip operations, non-adherence to procedures, lack of foresight and the taking of unnecessary risks, often in quite simple operations. Subtitle: internal evaluation /
Responsibility: International Labour Office.
Abstract/Summary: OBJECTIVES: Build the organizational capacity of rural women to manage their production and to address their socio-economic needs; establish and develop a sustainable savings and credit mechanism appropriate for poor rural women; strengthen the capacity of the implementing agency to implement, monitor, evaluate and document a replicable model of district assistance to rural women; develop income-generating activities for rural women. FINDINGS: The evaluation of Phase II of the project found that the previous phase had had considerable impact on the women's capacity to sustain the activities initiated by the project. The women were meeting regularly to plan and discuss a variety of economic activities, including savings mobilization and credit repayments. They were using support services such as innovative agricultural technology and production related skills training provided by the project. However, crop failure had prevented them from repaying loans. More emphasis was, therefore, put in Phase II on the diversification of economic activities such as horticulture and off-farm production which yielded more stable incomes. One important effect of the strategy to empower the women was that about half of those elected to the Village Councils were beneficiaries of the project. They had, therefore, substantially increased their participation in decision-making at the village-level as a direct result of the project.

Responsibility: Nelien Haspels, Zaitun Mohamed Kasim, Constance Thomas and Deirdre McCann ; International Labour Office, ILO Bangkok Area Office and East Asia Multidisciplinary Advisory Team.
Abstract/Summary: The reasons for eliminating sexual harassment in the workplace are thus both human and economic.  This book is intended to help show how attitudical changes in society at large, legislation and appropriate workplace training, information and management can all contribute to overcome the incidence of sexual harassment. It also reflects a great diversity of opinion and approach to the issues involved with sexual harassment, it is all the more striking that such unequivocal and committed agreement is emerging worldwide on basis points such as: (a) no woman or man of any age should have to tolerare such conduct; (b) every employer and worker should take appropriate measures aimed at preventing and eliminating sexual harassment; and (c) it is in both the social and economic interests of society as a whole to suppress such behaviour.

Main Title: Action against sexual harassment at work in Asia and the Pacific /
Responsibility: Nelien Haspels, Zaitun Mohamed Kasim, Constance Thomas and Deirdre McCann ; International Labour Office, ILO Bangkok Area Office and East Asia Multidisciplinary Advisory Team.
Abstract/Summary: The reasons for eliminating sexual harassment in the workplace are thus both human and economic.  This book is intended to help show how attitudical changes in society at large, legislation and appropriate workplace training, information and management can all contribute to overcome the incidence of sexual harassment. It also reflects a great diversity of opinion and approach to the issues involved with sexual harassment, it is all the more striking that such unequivocal and committed agreement is emerging worldwide on basis points such as: (a) no woman or man of any age should have to tolerare such conduct; (b) every employer and worker should take appropriate measures aimed at preventing and eliminating sexual harassment; and (c) it is in both the social and economic interests of society as a whole to suppress such behaviour.

Main Title: Africa's crises :
Subtitle: recent analysis of armed conflicts and natural disasters in Africa /
Responsibility: P. Dunne and G. Mhone ; InFocus Programme on Crisis Response and Reconstruction, Recovery and Reconstruction Department, International Labour Office.
Corporate Author: InFocus Programme on Crisis Response and Reconstruction.
Abstract/Summary: Comprises two papers that examine challenges to the ILO's Decent Work Agenda in Africa. The first paper examines the socio-economic aspects of crisis caused by armed conflict through case studies of Mozambique and Rwanda. The second paper reviews the impact of natural disasters on livelihoods in Africa south of the Sahara and assesses the nature of the interventions implemented. Presents case studies of natural disasters in Mozambique and South Africa.

Main Title: The Agreement on Textiles and Clothing :
Subtitle: potential effects on gendered employment in Pakistan /
Responsibility: Karin Astrid Siegmann; International Labour Office.
Abstract/Summary: Because the system of “purdah” has translated into extreme occupational segregation, a disproportionately large share of Pakistan’s female labour force is employed in stitching/sewing made-ups and garments for export. But since January 2005, when the ATC terminated the decades-old quota regime that regulated international trade in textiles and clothing, the industry has been restructuring to cope with the consequences of increased competition on the newly liberalized global market. Siegmann argues that Pakistan’s competitive position is likely to suffer most in the made-ups/garments segment of the market. If so, women could eventually suffer disproportionate job losses with scant prospects for alternative employment.

Main Title: The Agreement on Textiles and Clothing :
Subtitle: potential effects on gendered employment in Pakistan /
Responsibility: Karin Astrid Siegmann; International Labour Office.
Abstract/Summary: Because the system of “purdah” has translated into extreme occupational segregation, a disproportionately large share of Pakistan’s female labour force is employed in stitching/sewing made-ups and garments for export. But since January 2005, when the ATC terminated the decades-old quota regime that regulated international trade in textiles and clothing, the industry has been restructuring to cope with the consequences of increased competition on the newly liberalized global market. Siegmann argues that Pakistan’s competitive position is likely to suffer most in the made-ups/garments segment of the market. If so, women could eventually suffer disproportionate job losses with scant prospects for alternative employment.

Main Title: ACTRAV policy note on green jobs
Responsibility: ACTRAV, International Labour Office.
Abstract/Summary: ACTRAV sees the ILO’s work on “green jobs” as an element of its work to address employment and labour market issues in relation to climate changes and new ways of production. The direct effects of climate change as well as the indirect effects of adaptation and mitigation measures to address climate changes have an enormous impact on employment and the labour market.With a wide definition of green jobs we might say that all jobs that contribute to environmentally sustainable development are green. It might span over all sectors and industries and cover all workers as not only new jobs in the “clean” energy sector are developing, but also when other sectors are getting “greener”.In the process of transition to a low carbon society it is important not to forget the social dimension of sustainable development.The “greening” of the economy is a positive step towards a cleaner planet. With the right social policy framework it should also lead to an economy that works for all – an economy which includes decent work and workers' rights as international requirements in a worldwide climate change regime.

Main Title: Agricultural value chain development :
Subtitle: threat or opportunity for women's employment? /
Responsibility: prepared by Daniela Bolzani,  Soline de Villard and Jennie Dey de Pryck ; International Labour Office (ILO); Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO) ; International Fund for Agricultural Development (IFAD).
Abstract/Summary: Shows that agricultural markets are rapidly globalizing, generating new consumption patterns and new production and distribution systems. Demonstrates that value chains, often controlled by multinational or national firms and supermarkets, are capturing a growing share of the agri-food systems in developing regions. Argues that they can provide opportunities for quality employment for men and women, yet they can also be channels to transfer costs and risks to the weakest nodes, particularly women. Affirms that they often perpetuate gender stereotypes that keep women in lower paid, casual work and do not necessarily lead to greater gender equality.

Main Title: Addressing inequities in access to health care for vulnerable groups in countries of the European region
Responsibility: Xenia Scheil-Adlung and Catharina Kuhl ; International Labour Office, Social Security Department.
Abstract/Summary: This paper highlights issues that contribute to persisting inequalities in access to health care of vulnerable groups in the broader European region, including countries of the European Union (EU), the Commonwealth of Independent States (CIS), Central and Eastern Europe (CEE), and selected countries of Central Asia. It focusses on key determinants of vulnerability, primarily labour market impacts such as income, employment, and related gaps in access to social protection, and to social health protection in particular. Moreover, it concentrates on stratification of vulnerability looking at specific population groups, including women, the elderly, migrants, and ethnic groups, mainly Roma, who are often disadvantaged in regard with determinants of vulnerability as focused on here. Special attention is also given to rural and urban inequities.

Main Title: The administration and financing of paid sick leave /
Responsibility: Anke Schliwen, Alison Earle, Jeff Hayes and S. Jody Heymann; International Labour Office.
Abstract/Summary: Decision-makers in countries considering adoption or reform of paid sick leave policies need to understand the mechanisms used to finance sick leave provision. Using global data on sick leave legislation, the authors find the type of model used – employer liability, social insurance, social assistance, or a combination thereof – has significant implications for the duration and generosity of sickness benefits. However, they find no significant relationship between the duration or generosity of sick leave and economic indicators such as per capita GDP, unemployment rates, or national competitiveness. Potential mechanisms enabling both effective sick leave policies and strong economic outcomes are discussed.

These include building capacity for generating and analyzing labour market information, increasing training and employment opportunities for women and men, building a conducive environment for micro and small enterprises and enhancing the economic empowerment of vulnerable groups (especially women, youth, ex-combatants, returning migrants and people with disabilities).

Main Title: The administration and financing of paid sick leave /
Responsibility: Anke Schliwen, Alison Earle, Jeff Hayes and S. Jody Heymann; International Labour Office.
Abstract/Summary: Decision-makers in countries considering adoption or reform of paid sick leave policies need to understand the mechanisms used to finance sick leave provision. Using global data on sick leave legislation, the authors find the type of model used – employer liability, social insurance, social assistance, or a combination thereof – has significant implications for the duration and generosity of sickness benefits. However, they find no significant relationship between the duration or generosity of sick leave and economic indicators such as per capita GDP, unemployment rates, or national competitiveness. Potential mechanisms enabling both effective sick leave policies and strong economic outcomes are discussed.

Main Title: Active labour market policies in Spain :
Subtitle: a macroeconomic evaluation /
Responsibility: José María Arranz, Carlos García Serrano and Virginia Hernanz; International Labour Office.
Abstract/Summary: Using aggregate panel data on Spain’s 17 regions for the period 1987–2010, the authors present a macroeconomic assessment of a variety of active labour market policies, including employment subsidies for permanent contracts, job-creation schemes and vocational training programmes. Their results suggest that employment subsidies for permanent contracts had no notable effect on aggregate levels of permanent or temporary employment. However, they do appear to have had a small positive effect on transitions from unemployment to employment, and from temporary to permanent employment, particularly since the 1997 labour reform. Better targeted subsidies, the authors conclude, would have incurred fewer deadweight and substitution effects.

Subtitle: an analytical report /
Responsibility: International Labour Organization.
Abstract/Summary: The report shows that even though policies do not exclude people living with HIV, they face challenges in accessing the existing social protection services, notably a lack of adequate knowledge about the programmes, complicated procedures for accessing programmes, and stigma and discrimination. With the possible exception of medical services, social protection access for PLHIV and key populations is wanting in the four countries. Data from the studies suggest that a combination of income, livelihood and employment support is needed, in addition to health services, for social protection to have a bigger impact on key HIV populations – and the global literature review supports this point. As well, coordinated efforts involving ministries of labour, employers and the private sector and trade unions, ministries of health and gender, national AIDS programmes, social protection programmes, civil society organizations and PLHIV organizations is needed to address existing barriers and increase the proportion of PLHIV with access to and benefits from social assistance and other forms of social protection.

Main Title: An accuracy assessment of the global employment trends' unemployment rate forecasts
Responsibility: Evangelia Bourmpoula and Christina Wieser ; International Labour Office, Research Department.
Abstract/Summary: This study provides a quantitative assessment of the bias, accuracy, and efficiency of the Global Employment Trends (GET) global and regional unemployment rate forecasts made in three recent annual GET reports. After conducting a series of statistical tests, the results suggest that, on average across all countries with data availability, the GET unemployment rate forecasts are slightly biased; we over-predict one and two years ahead and under-predict three and four years ahead. However, this bias is not significant for one to three years ahead. Moreover, our tests for accuracy show that the shorter the prediction period, the more accurate our forecasts indicated by smaller forecast errors for shorter prediction periods and larger forecast errors for longer periods.

Main Title: Achieving equal employment opportunities for people with disabilities through legislation :
Subtitle: guidelines /
Responsibility: International Labour Office.
Abstract/Summary: These guidelines were first published in 2007, with the support of the Government of Ireland, shortly after the adoption of the United Nations Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (CRPD), to promote the reappraisal of disability as a human rights issue. Intended primarily for policy-makers and drafters of legislation, particularly in States Parties to the CRPD that are required to review their legislative provisions as a first step, they aim to support the revision of existing laws and development of new laws of relevance to women and men with disabilities, and the development of regulations and policies to give effect to these laws. Focusing on key concepts underlying international standards – equality of opportunity, non-discrimination, equal treatment of men and women - and providing examples of law and practice in countries around the world, the guidelines are updated in this second edition, drawing on the rapid developments that have taken place in national legislation as well as implementation and enforcement measures since 2008.

Main Title: Achieving scale and efficiency in microinsurance through retail and banking correspondents
Responsibility: Jeremy Leach, Anand Menon and Sandisiwe Ncube ; International Labour Organization, Impact Insurance.
Abstract/Summary: Across the world, insurers have shifted away from traditional agents and brokers to partner with various alternative distribution channels – ranging from churches, trade unions and mobile network operators to banks, retailers and even civil service organizations - as a way of reaching critical mass at low cost. This study examines two of these channels – banking and retail correspondents – studying four examples in four countries.

Main Title: Active labour market policies in Spain :
Subtitle: a macroeconomic evaluation /
Responsibility: José María Arranz, Carlos García Serrano and Virginia Hernanz; International Labour Office.
Abstract/Summary: Using aggregate panel data on Spain’s 17 regions for the period 1987–2010, the authors present a macroeconomic assessment of a variety of active labour market policies, including employment subsidies for permanent contracts, job-creation schemes and vocational training programmes. Their results suggest that employment subsidies for permanent contracts had no notable effect on aggregate levels of permanent or temporary employment. However, they do appear to have had a small positive effect on transitions from unemployment to employment, and from temporary to permanent employment, particularly since the 1997 labour reform. Better targeted subsidies, the authors conclude, would have incurred fewer deadweight and substitution effects.

Responsibility: International Labour Office, Social Protection Department.
Abstract/Summary: This policy paper: (i) examines the dimensions of the global health crisis based on severe deficits in health protection and limited access to needed health care; (ii) presents the extent of the health crisis at global, regional and national level as well as rural/urban divergences within countries and their root causes; (iii) suggests policy options to address the health protection crisis using the framework of national social protection floors by focusing on inclusive legislation and adequate financing as well as making quality services available and providing financial protection; (iv) concludes that progressing towards universal health protection is possible by developing a three step approach that yields highest rates of returns in terms of sustainability, economic growth and equity. The Annexes present global data on total health expenditure, health coverageand skilled health workers for 171 countries.

Subtitle: policies to get immigrants into middle-skilled work in Europe /
Responsibility: Meghan Benton, Madelaine Sumption, Kristine Alsvik, Susan Fratzke, Christiane Kuptsch, and Demetrios G. Papademetriou ; Migration Policy Institute (MPI), International Labour Office.
Abstract/Summary: Examines how recent immigrants in Europe are failing to move from low-skilled precarious jobs into decent work. While some countries have invested in labour market integration policies, they have focused primarily on getting immigrants into work rather than to facilitate career progression.  Newly arrived immigrants often struggle as a result of limited language proficiency, discrimination, and difficulties in having qualifications recognized and signalling how skills and experience meet employer needs. Many experience protracted periods of inactivity, unemployment, or long tenures in low-skilled work.

Main Title: 2014 labour overview :
Publisher: ILO Regional Office for Latin America and the Caribbean,
Subtitle: Latin America and the Caribbean /
Responsibility: Regional Office for Latin America and the Caribbean, International Labour Office.
Corporate Author: ILO Regional Office for Latin America and the Caribbean.
Abstract/Summary: Currently, there are nearly 15 million unemployed in urban areas of the region. Next year, there will likely be 500,000 more unemployed since the urban unemployment rate is expected to rise by two-tenths of a percentage point, from the ILO’s estimate of 6.1% in 2014 to 6.3% in 2015. Economic growth estimates for the region have been diminishing since the beginning of the year and are now expected to reach just over 1.0% in 2014 and 2.2% in 2015. This growth will be insufficient to prevent an increase in unemployment.

Main Title: 2015 labour overview :
Publisher: ILO Regional Office for Latin America and the Caribbean,
Subtitle: Latin America and the Caribbean /
Responsibility: Regional Office for Latin America and the Caribbean, International Labour Office.
Abstract/Summary: The most obvious indicator is the increase in the national unemployment rate, which will reach an estimated 6.7% in 2015, as compared with 6.2% in 2014. This means that at the end of 2015, there will be 1.7 million newly unemployed in the region. Some indicators of quality of employment remain positive, such as real wages and social protection coverage, largely as a result of recent institutional advances. In the near future, however, these may be affected by the decline in wage employment as a share of total employment.

Main Title: The 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development /
Responsibility: International Labour Office, Governing Body, 325th Session, Geneva, Nov. 2015 ; Institutional Section.
Abstract/Summary: This document summarizes key features of the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development, discusses their implications for the Organization and proposes points for decision regarding ILO follow-up action in the context of the end to poverty centenary initiative in the run-up to the 2019 session of the International Labour Conference, including the preparation of the Strategic Plan for 2018–21. The adoption of the 2030 Agenda has far-reaching implications for the Organization – constituents and Office. The ILO has a major opportunity to further the goals it set itself in the 2008 Declaration on Social Justice for a Fair Globalization. The integrated approach of the Agenda calls for both a renewed drive by the ILO to offer constituents normative-based policy packages across the four pillars of decent work that are specific to country circumstances and closer collaboration with partner agencies. The ILO’s long experience of tripartite partnership is a major advantage as the UN system seeks to develop the concept of multi-stakeholder partnerships around the Agenda (see draft decision in paragraph 44).

Subtitle: agricultural value chains : summary report /
Responsibility: International Labour Organization, ILO Office for Afghanistan.
Abstract/Summary: The main thrust of the report deals with the competiveness situation (erosion and gains) in the five sectors under review. The study found that, with the exception of the raisins sector, the selected agricultural sectors in Afghanistan suffered from a loss of competitiveness vis-à-vis those of Pakistan and/or Iran during the 2007-12 period: where Afghanistan’s lowest price was caught up by a competitor in rice and tomato sectors, and Afghanistan completely lost price competitiveness in wheat and potato sectors. This would have direct impact on trade given Afghanistan’s highly liberal tariff regime.

Main Title: Ageing and labour market implications for Lithuania
Responsibility: Ronald Bouman, Richard Horne, Santo Milasi and Naren Prasad ; International Labour Office, Research Department.
Abstract/Summary: Due to low fertility rates, higher life expectancies and substantial outward migration, Lithuania is one of the fastest ageing countries in Europe. As a result, the working-age population is expected to decline dramatically, giving rise to a number of economic, labour market and social challenges. Inorder to mitigate the effects of ageing, policies are needed to raise the participation rates of older workers. This includes placing greater emphasis on active labour market programmes and other measures to ensure older workers are given the opportunity and skills to return to work and remain attached to the job market. Social dialogue and tripartite discussion are vital to successful policy design as well as generating the public support to implement the necessary reforms. In addition, a number of complementary policy options will also be merited to fully attenuate the downside risks, notably by increasing the productive capacity of the core working-age population through enhanced labour productivity, increased participation of all groups and addressing the root causes of emigration. This working paper discusses theses labour market implications in more detail and provides international policy examples on how best to address these challenges.

Newly arrived immigrants often struggle as a result of limited language proficiency, discrimination, and difficulties in having qualifications recognized and signalling how skills and experience meet employer needs. Many experience protracted periods of inactivity, unemployment, or long tenures in low-skilled work.

Main Title: Alternative labour protection movements in the United States :
Subtitle: reshaping industrial relations? /
Responsibility: Janice Fine ; International Labour Office.
Abstract/Summary: The United States is one of the developed countries that have experienced the steepest declines of unionization and collective bargaining in recent decades. Its traditional industrial relations institutions, premised on the prevalence of “standard” employment relationships, have long been eroded by restrictive legislation and employer opposition. Meanwhile, precarious employment, sub-standard conditions and marginalization have become widespread features of the labour market, leading to the spontaneous emergence of alternative, often community-based initiatives to protect vulnerable workers using highly innovative strategies. “Worker centres”, in particular, have been very active to that end, often teaming up with formal trade unions to pursue their objectives.

Main Title: An alternative to migration
Subtitle: building roads and bridges /
Responsibility: International Labour Organisation.
Abstract/Summary: The 7.8-magnitude earthquake which struck Nepal on 25 April 2015, killed nearly 9,000 people, destroyed part of the country’s infrastructure and left millions homeless. With financial support from the World Bank, the ILO assisted the Government of Nepal with one of the country’s largest infrastructure project programmes – building bridges and upgrading, rehabilitating and maintaining local road networks and river crossings in 37 districts, targeting more than 15 million people living in these districts. The project is expected to create 3.9 million days of paid decent jobs.

Main Title: Analysis of employment, real wage, and productivity trends in South Africa since 1994 /
Responsibility: by Martin Wittenberg ; International Labour Office, Inclusive Labour Markets, Labour Relations and Working Conditions Branch.
Abstract/Summary: South Africa’s transition to democracy in 1994 engendered expectations of rapid changes in racial and gender disparities in wages. Twenty years after the transition there are strong disagreements about whether there have been gains and how these have been distributed. In particular there are debates about whether wage increases have outstripped productivity increases.

Main Title: Analytical report on the international labour migration statistics database in ASEAN :
Subtitle: improving data collection for evidence-based policy-making /
Responsibility: Tripartite Action for the Protection and Promotion of the Rights of  Migrant Workers in the ASEAN Region (ASEAN TRIANGLE project), ILO Regional Office for Asia and the Pacific.
Corporate Author: ILO Regional Office for Asia and the Pacific.
Abstract/Summary: The report provides a broad-based quantitative analysis of the current and emerging trends in international labour migration into, from, and among the ten Member States of the Association of Southeast Asian Nations (ASEAN). The report analyses some of the most common drivers underpinning these trends and argues that labour migration is likely to increase within the region over the short and medium term. The report’s key findings are drawn from the national level data collected through the International Labour Organization’s (ILO) International Labour Migration Statistics (ILMS) Database in ASEAN. Its recommendations draw upon these findings as well as the available meta-data, to identify solutions and key directions for building a stronger evidence base to help improve policies on international labour migration in ASEAN.

Main Title: Andemic informality
Subtitle: assessing labor informality, employment, and income risk in the Andes /
Responsibility: Edwin A. Goñi Pacchioni
Abstract/Summary: Recent studies of Latin American labor markets have focused on analysis of the determinants, evolution, and implications of increasing informal arrangements between workers and employers. This book adds to that tradition with a refreshed dynamic and causal perspective that exploits novel panel data sets, recent methodological advances, and identification strategies after recent policy reforms in Andean countries.

Main Title: Annual evaluation report 2013 - 2014 /
Responsibility: International Labour Office, Evaluation Office.
Abstract/Summary: Since 2011, the Annual Evaluation Report (AER) has reported on the performance of ILO’s evaluation function as measured against its result-based strategy and has reflected on selected aspects of the Office’s overall effectiveness. Part I of this report deals with progress in implementing the three outcomes identified in the result-based evaluation strategy. Part II assesses the ILO’s overall effectiveness in implementing the Strategic Policy Framework (SPF). The report also reports on its biennial stock-taking workshop which took place in November 2013.

Publisher: ILO Regional Office for Latin America and the Caribbean,
Subtitle: Latin America and the Caribbean /
Responsibility: Regional Office for Latin America and the Caribbean, International Labour Office.
Abstract/Summary: The most obvious indicator is the increase in the national unemployment rate, which will reach an estimated 6.7% in 2015, as compared with 6.2% in 2014. This means that at the end of 2015, there will be 1.7 million newly unemployed in the region. Some indicators of quality of employment remain positive, such as real wages and social protection coverage, largely as a result of recent institutional advances. In the near future, however, these may be affected by the decline in wage employment as a share of total employment.

Subtitle: 33rd compliance synthesis report, Cambodia /
Responsibility: Better Factories Cambodia, International Labour Office ; International Finance Corporation.
Abstract/Summary: The latest results of public reporting point out that since the start of the initiative, the percentage of low compliant factories has dropped from 10% at the start of public reporting to a little bit over 4% of all factories included in the database. In addition, the percentage of factories complying with all 21 critical issues has increased from 28% before public reporting to 47% after public reporting, among the factories covered in the database in the past year. Although there are some individual critical issues that have not improved, overall it can be concluded that the public reporting initiative is driving change at the factory level.

Main Title: The 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development /
Responsibility: International Labour Office, Governing Body, 325th Session, Geneva, Nov. 2015 ; Institutional Section.
Abstract/Summary: This document summarizes key features of the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development, discusses their implications for the Organization and proposes points for decision regarding ILO follow-up action in the context of the end to poverty centenary initiative in the run-up to the 2019 session of the International Labour Conference, including the preparation of the Strategic Plan for 2018–21. The adoption of the 2030 Agenda has far-reaching implications for the Organization – constituents and Office. The ILO has a major opportunity to further the goals it set itself in the 2008 Declaration on Social Justice for a Fair Globalization. The integrated approach of the Agenda calls for both a renewed drive by the ILO to offer constituents normative-based policy packages across the four pillars of decent work that are specific to country circumstances and closer collaboration with partner agencies. The ILO’s long experience of tripartite partnership is a major advantage as the UN system seeks to develop the concept of multi-stakeholder partnerships around the Agenda (see draft decision in paragraph 44).

Main Title: Advancing financial inclusion through access to insurance
Subtitle: the role of postal networks /
Responsibility: Guilherme Suedekum ; International Labour Organization, Impact Insurance.
Abstract/Summary: With 1.5 billion people worldwide getting access to financial services through a post office, postal networks are powerful tools to advance financial inclusion. Posts possess or can develop characteristics such as client understanding, product diversification, national outreach, rural advantage, and trust with clients that have the potential to transform postal networks into well-suited providers of insurance. Insurance is a relatively easy way for posts to diversify their sources of revenue.

Main Title: Advancing gender equality :
Subtitle: the co-operative way /
Responsibility: International Labour Office, Enterprises Department.
Abstract/Summary: The adoption of the Beijing Declaration and Platform for Action at the Fourth World Conference on Women in 1995 laid the foundation for action towards gender equality in all aspects of economic, social, cultural and political life. Twenty years later, the International Labour Organization (ILO) and the International Co-operative Alliance (Alliance) have joined together to assess how one particular sector-the co-operative movement—is impacting (and is impacted by) progress towards women’s empowerment and gender equality.

Main Title: Afghan competitiveness for job creation
Subtitle: agricultural value chains : summary report /
Responsibility: International Labour Organization, ILO Office for Afghanistan.
Abstract/Summary: The main thrust of the report deals with the competiveness situation (erosion and gains) in the five sectors under review. The study found that, with the exception of the raisins sector, the selected agricultural sectors in Afghanistan suffered from a loss of competitiveness vis-à-vis those of Pakistan and/or Iran during the 2007-12 period: where Afghanistan’s lowest price was caught up by a competitor in rice and tomato sectors, and Afghanistan completely lost price competitiveness in wheat and potato sectors. This would have direct impact on trade given Afghanistan’s highly liberal tariff regime.
