Main Title: A 10-question pocket guide to making MSD work for you
Responsibility: International Labour Organization.
Abstract/Summary: This pocket guide aims to help front-line practitioners feel more comfortable in their understanding of what the MSD approach is and broadly how it can be practiced. It presents ten questions to stimulate reflection and help practitioners chart their own course through an MSD intervention lifecycle.This is not a comprehensive guide. Instead, it intends to communicate the main substance of the approach in a digestible manner, contributing towards a common understanding within project teams and facilitating further conversation among project stakeholders. Individual team members may choose to use it as a personal checklist or to foster team-wide discussions. It may be used in real-time or employed in a forward- or backward-looking manner.

Main Title: Advancing a safe and healthy working environment and decent work in the tanning and leather industry
Responsibility: International Labour Organization.
Abstract/Summary: This ILO brief highlights the significance and challenges of the global tanning and leather industry. This centuries-old industry produces a wide range of goods, from high-end fashion items to everyday consumer products, and plays a crucial role in the global economy, with prominent production hubs in Asia, Europe, and the Americas. The industry faces decent work challenges, including poor working conditions, low wages, and health risks, particularly in developing countries. The ILO emphasizes the need for a multi-faceted approach to address these issues, combining regulatory frameworks, investment in skills and technology, adoption of sustainable practices, and robust social dialogue. The brief also underscores the importance of improving occupational safety and health (OSH) to protect workers from biological, chemical, physical, and ergonomic hazards. Collaboration among governments, employers, and workers is essential to ensure the industry's long-term viability and contribution to decent work and sustainable development.

Main Title: Advancing decent work in supply chains
Subtitle: decent work challenges and opportunities in the textiles and clothing sector /
Responsibility: International Labour Organization.
Abstract/Summary: This ILO brief highlights the critical role of the textiles and clothing industries in global economic and employment growth. These sectors provide jobs to over 90 million people, predominantly women, and are undergoing significant transformations driven by technological advancements, sustainability imperatives, and changing consumer preferences. The brief emphasizes the need for governments, employers, and workers to collaborate in addressing decent work deficits, such as poor working conditions, low wages, and gender inequalities. It also underscores the importance of promoting skills development, lifelong learning, and sustainable practices to navigate the sector's rapid changes and ensure a just transition for all workers. The ILO's initiatives, including the Better Work Programme and the adoption of international labour standards, are crucial in advancing decent work and social justice in the textiles and clothing sector.

Main Title: Advancing decent work in the sugarcane supply chain
Responsibility: International Labour Organization.
Abstract/Summary: This ILO brief from March 2025 discusses advancing Decent Work in the sugarcane supply chain, highlighting the industry's growth driven by technological advancements and rising demand for sugar and bioethanol. The sugar supply chain involves diverse stakeholders, from smallholder farmers to large agribusinesses, with sugarcane dominating global sugar production. Key players like Brazil, India, and the EU significantly influence the market, with Brazil being the largest exporter. The industry faces challenges such as decent work deficits, including lack of workers' rights and inadequate working conditions, particularly in the upstream segments. Efforts to address these issues include formalization and mechanization initiatives, as well as projects aimed at enhancing labour standards and promoting gender equality. The brief also emphasizes the importance of international instruments and national legislations in promoting decent work and sustainable practices within the sugarcane supply chain.

Evidence from over 200 evaluation reports presented in this paper can help ILO practitioners, governments, employers’, and workers’ organizations design more effective, resilient projects that position disability inclusion as a foundation for sustainable development and long-term impact.

Main Title: Advancing social justice in a changing world of work
Subtitle: addressing megatrends, evolving policy developments and other drivers of change through sectoral social dialogue /
Responsibility: International Labour Organization.
Abstract/Summary: This ILO brief focuses on addressing megatrends and policy developments that are reshaping the global labor landscape. The publication highlights the International Labour Organization's (ILO) efforts to promote decent work and social justice through sectoral social dialogue, involving employers, workers, and governments. It discusses key drivers of change such as demographic shifts, climate change, technological advances, and globalization, and their impact on labor markets and supply chains. The brief emphasizes the importance of strong institutions and inclusive social dialogue mechanisms in navigating these challenges. It also outlines the role of international labor standards and policy instruments, such as free trade agreements and corporate sustainability due diligence, in advancing decent work. The brief aims to enhance the knowledge and capacity of European sectoral social partners to promote resilient, inclusive, and sustainable sectors and supply chains.

Responsibility: [ILO Regional Office for Asia and the Pacific, International Labour Organization].

Abstract/Summary: The findings and recommendations identify opportunities to further strengthen efforts to combat forced labour and child labour in Malaysia, particularly in the E&E sector. Malaysia’s electrical and electronics (E&E) sector is a global leader, contributing significantly to the nation’s economic growth and development. At the same time, the sector is not immune to challenges in labour practices, particularly in addressing vulnerabilities that may affect workers. Thus, this study is intended as a resource for policymakers, businesses, and other stakeholders to inspire action and collaboration.

Responsibility: [Megan Cherewick and Miranda Dally ; Labour Administration, Labour Inspection and Occupational Safety and Health Branch (LABADMIN/OSH), Safety + Health for All], Vision Zero Fund, International Labour Organization.

Viet Nam is ranked sixth among all countries in the world affected by climate variability and extreme weather. Given Viet Nam’s substantial agricultural sector, understanding climate change-related hazards experienced by agricultural workers is essential to safeguarding safety, health and well-being of agricultural workers.

It highlights critical gaps in land, housing, and livelihoods, and offers targeted recommendations to strengthen rehabilitation, expand social protection, and prevent forced and child labour.

Main Title: Artificial intelligence adoption and its impact on jobs
Subtitle: paper prepared under South Africa’s G20 Presidency /
Responsibility: Framework Working Group; International Labour Organization.
Abstract/Summary: This paper, drawing primarily upon recent and ongoing research at the ILO, suggests a landscape where AI is more likely to augment human capabilities and enhance productivity in many roles rather than leading to widespread automation, although certain occupations and demographic groups exhibit higher exposure to transformative effects. Using an innovative and refined methodology, the paper begins by examining potential occupational exposure to GenAI in the G20, refining previous estimates to identify varying gradients of impact and highlighting significant disparities based on gender and national/regional income levels. The analysis then contextualizes these AI-specific potentials by exploring the current digital intensity of employment and the realized employment effects of a wider array of emerging digital technologies, underscoring the heterogeneous nature of digital transformation across different economic contexts. Furthermore, the paper touches on important aspects of AI's influence on job quality, including the rise of algorithmic management and the often-overlooked role of the "data labourers" who underpin AI systems.

Main Title: Assessing the role of employers in improving labour migration governance in Uganda
Responsibility: by Martin Luther Munu ; [ILO Country Office for Ethiopia, Djibouti, Somalia, Sudan and South Sudan].
Corporate Author: ILO Country Office for Ethiopia, Djibouti, Somalia, Sudan and South Sudan.,
Abstract/Summary: This report offers an evidence-based guidance and recommendation on how employers contribute to improving the governance of labour migration. It also highlights the growing importance of private employment agencies (PrEAs), many of whom are not yet affiliated with employer organizations such as FUE to affiliate with such organizations. The report draws attention to challenges in recruitment and underscores the need for stronger regulation, better collaboration, and more meaningful employer engagement in both policy formulation and implementation.

Main Title: Assessment for designing an e-formalization platform for the informal economy
Subtitle: formalization of the informal economy project /
Responsibility: Altansukh Myagmarjav and Amartuvshin Orgodol ; Government of Mongolia, Ministry of Family, Labour and Social Protection, International Labour Organization.
Abstract/Summary: This assessment report provides a process analysis of current government services formalizing the informal economy. Digital services have been applied widely to make government services faster and more easily accessible, thereby significantly reducing the challenges and bureaucratic delays. The government also established a unified national infrastructure for information exchange, including electronic platforms, such as e-mongolia.mn, e-business.mn, etax.mta.mn, ndaatgal.mn, and ejob.gov.mn. These are comprehensive digital services for citizens and businesses. However, the report offers suggestions for continuous development and improvement for some newly launched systems, such as the e-business and e-job platforms. Additionally improving collaboration and integration between these public and private systems will make the formalization process more efficient, ensuring that informal workers and businesses can transition smoothly to the formal economy.

Main Title: Assessment of drivers and constraints to occupational safety and health improvement in the sugar cane supply chain in Colombia
Responsibility: [by Schneider Guataqui Cervera, Yuber Liliana Rodríguez Rojas and Juan Carlos Munevar Pérez ; Labour Administration, Labour Inspection and Occupational Safety and Health Branch (LABADMIN/OSH), Safety + Health for All, International Labour Organization], Vision Zero Fund.
Abstract/Summary: This study identifies nine key incentives to strengthen a culture of prevention, improve training, and ensure compliance with occupational safety and health (OSH) regulations, as well as five limitations that still hinder effective implementation, such as lack of awareness of the legislation, climate-related challenges, and limited access to specialized technical training. Based on these findings, the study proposes six concrete intervention models to improve OSH in the sector, using a comprehensive approach that engages public, private, and labor actors to promote decent work and sustainable development.

Main Title: Assessment of gender equality and social inclusion gaps in the skills and TVET system of Bangladesh
Subtitle: subtitle /
Responsibility: [ILO Country Office for Bangladesh], International Labour Organization.
Abstract/Summary: This brief highlights the main point from the report on the need for significant improvements in gender equality and social inclusion (GESI) within Bangladesh's Technical and Vocational Education and Training (TVET) system through a coordinated approach. involving various stakeholders.

Main Title: Assessment of the legal frameworks for access to complaints and remedy procedures by migrant workers in and from Kenya and Uganda
Responsibility: By Virginia A. Nelder ; [ILO Country Office for Ethiopia, Djibouti, Somalia, Sudan and South Sudan], International Labour Organization.
Corporate Author: ILO Country Office for Ethiopia, Djibouti, Somalia, Sudan and South Sudan.,
Abstract/Summary: This assessment examines the legal and institutional frameworks in Uganda and Kenya, two major origin countries for migrant workers in the East and Horn of Africa region. It evaluates the extent to which their domestic systems uphold migrant workers’ rights to access justice and how these align with regional and international standards. Through desk research and stakeholder consultations, the report identifies strengths, gaps, and areas for legal procedural reform.

Main Title: Attained vs. effective schooling
Subtitle: assessing educational mismatch using PIAAC data /
Responsibility: Inés P. Murillo Huertas and Josep Lluís Raymond Bara ; International Labour Organization.
Abstract/Summary: This article proposes a measure of educational mismatch based on a novel definition ofschooling that takes into account the different levels of skills acquired by individuals with the sameeducation. It is assumed that less (more) able individuals could compensate for their skill deficit(surplus) with more (less) education, and PIAAC data are used to analyse the substitution betweenskill and educational mismatches in terms of wages. We use the proposed measure to assess thelevel of effective educational mismatch in a set of countries. When accounting for workers’ realskills, we find that overeducation tends to drop, the difference between observed and effectiveeducational mismatch varying by country.

Main Title: Attaining and improving profitability and sustainability in inclusive insurance
Subtitle: learning from the leaders working paper series /
Responsibility: [by Lisa Morgan and Karimi Nthiga ; Impact Insurance Facility], International Labour Organization.
Abstract/Summary: The purpose of inclusive insurance is to build the financial resilience of underserved market segments, such as small businesses, smallholder farmers, and low-income households. This goal can only be achieved if schemes are profitable or financially sustainable. From research and interviews with some of today’s leading inclusive insurers, this paper unpacks what it takes for inclusive insurance programmes to be financially sustainable and, based on their experiences, synthesizes a best-practice tool: checklists of actions that can be taken to improve financial sustainability. This paper is part of the Learning from the Leaders series, with the goal of generating and sharing practical knowledge to overcome key barriers identified in inclusive insurance, promoting the adoption of innovative and sustainable solutions in the sector.

Main Title: Balancing the right to strike and other public interests
Subtitle: the importance of the status of the right to strike /
Responsibility: Petra Herzfeld Olsson and Shae Mccrystal ; International Labour Organization.
Abstract/Summary: The regulation of strike action involves the balancing of competing public interests – the benefits derived from collective bargaining and the right to strike, and others including public safety, health, welfare and industrial peace. We explore how this balancing act plays out in the legal systems of two national jurisdictions, Australia and Sweden, and through the ILO principles on freedom of association as developed by the ILO’s supervisory bodies. We seek to understand how different regulatory approaches to these competing public interests produces different outcomes, and the potential consequences where protecting other public interests is prioritised over the right to strike.

Main Title: Bargaining on the frontline
Subtitle: what role did collective bargaining play in protecting/advancing the interests of frontline workers during the Coronavirus pandemic? /
Responsibility: Jill Rubery, Isabel Távora, Abbie Winton, Eva Herman and Alejandro Castillo ; International Labour Organization.
Abstract/Summary: Drawing on 12 case studies across ten countries of how trade unions and collective-bargaining institutions supported frontline workers in health, social care and food retail, this article finds that pre-existing or new collective bargaining or social dialogue forums provided important avenues for employee voice on pandemic management. Trade unions also supported marginalised frontline workers through multiple tactics, though most initiatives predated the pandemic and often depended upon gaining active state support which was not always possible. Trade unions were thus pursuing sword-of-justice objectives, though they were sometimes less open to revaluing frontline work already covered by collectively-negotiated grading structures.

Main Title: Barriers to the full digitization of wage payments in Chinese supply chains
Subtitle: a rapid assessment /
Responsibility: ILO Country Office for China and Mongolia, International Labour Organization.
Corporate Author: ILO Country Office for China and Mongolia,
Abstract/Summary: While digital payments are the norm in China, some factories still pay wages in cash. This research explores the barriers to digital wage adoption, especially among older, less educated, and migrant workers. It finds that factory size, location, and operational costs impact payment methods, and concerns over trust, security, and limited understanding of taxes and social protection slow the transition. The report offers targeted recommendations for brands and factory managers to promote responsible adoption of digital wages.
